chunks.title: Fuzzy numbers and intervals
chunks.content: The slide introduces the concept of fuzzy numbers and their properties. It highlights that fuzzy numbers are widely used and defined on the real number set, mapping to the interval [0,1]. It outlines three key conditions that a fuzzy number must satisfy: being a normal fuzzy set, having closed intervals for every alpha-cut, and having a bounded support.
chunks.raw_text:
Fuzzy numbers and intervals

▶ Widely used
▶ It should be defined on ℝ

A : ℝ → [0, 1]

▶ Fuzzy number satisfies:

(i) A must be a normal fuzzy set;
(ii) αA must be a closed interval for every α ∈ (0, 1];
(iii) the support of A, 0+A, must be bounded.
chunks.embedding_text: The slide introduces the concept of fuzzy numbers and their properties. It highlights that fuzzy numbers are widely used and defined on the real number set, mapping to the interval [0,1]. It outlines three key conditions that a fuzzy number must satisfy: being a normal fuzzy set, having closed intervals for every alpha-cut, and having a bounded support.

chunks.content: A fuzzy set A on the real numbers ℝ that satisfies three conditions: (i) A must be a normal fuzzy set, (ii) αA must be a closed interval for every α ∈ (0,1], (iii) the support of A, 0+A, must be bounded.
chunks.embedding_text: Fuzzy number: A fuzzy set A on the real numbers ℝ that satisfies three conditions: (i) A must be a normal fuzzy set, (ii) αA must be a closed interval for every α ∈ (0,1], (iii) the support of A, 0+A, must be bounded.

chunks.content: A fuzzy set A where the supremum of its membership function is equal to 1.
chunks.embedding_text: Normal fuzzy set: A fuzzy set A where the supremum of its membership function is equal to 1.

chunks.content: The set of elements in the universal set whose degree of membership in A is at least α.
chunks.embedding_text: Alpha-cut (α-cut): The set of elements in the universal set whose degree of membership in A is at least α.

chunks.title: Fuzzy numbers
chunks.content: The slide provides a mathematical definition of fuzzy numbers, specifying their structure and the properties of the functions involved.
chunks.raw_text:
Fuzzy numbers

▶ All fuzzy number are of the following:

A(x) = { 1 for x ∈ [a, b]
       l(x) for x ∈ (-∞, a)
       r(x) for x ∈ (b, ∞) }

▶ Where l is a monotonic increasing continuous function and r is a monotonic decreasing continuous function
chunks.embedding_text: The slide provides a mathematical definition of fuzzy numbers, specifying their structure and the properties of the functions involved.

chunks.content: A fuzzy number is a convex normalized fuzzy set on the real line with a membership function A(x) that is piecewise continuous and defined by three intervals: [a, b], (-∞, a), and (b, ∞).
chunks.embedding_text: Fuzzy number: A fuzzy number is a convex normalized fuzzy set on the real line with a membership function A(x) that is piecewise continuous and defined by three intervals: [a, b], (-∞, a), and (b, ∞).

chunks.content: A function that assigns a degree of membership to each element in the real line, where the degree is 1 for x in [a, b], l(x) for x in (-∞, a), and r(x) for x in (b, ∞).
chunks.embedding_text: Membership function (A(x)): A function that assigns a degree of membership to each element in the real line, where the degree is 1 for x in [a, b], l(x) for x in (-∞, a), and r(x) for x in (b, ∞).

chunks.content: A monotonic increasing continuous function for x in (-∞, a).
chunks.embedding_text: l(x): A monotonic increasing continuous function for x in (-∞, a).

chunks.content: A monotonic decreasing continuous function for x in (b, ∞).
chunks.embedding_text: r(x): A monotonic decreasing continuous function for x in (b, ∞).

chunks.title: Fuzzy cardinality
chunks.content: The slide introduces the concept of fuzzy cardinality, distinguishing it from the cardinality of a fuzzy set. It provides a formula for fuzzy cardinality involving an alpha-cut of a fuzzy set.
chunks.raw_text:
Fuzzy cardinality

► Recall from a past session that cardinality of a fuzzy set is different from fuzzy cardinality which is:

|A̅|(|^αA|) = α

chunks.title: Linguistic variables
chunks.content: The slide introduces the concept of linguistic variables in fuzzy logic, explaining that fuzzy numbers represent values of these variables. It lists examples of linguistic variables with fuzzy states such as temperature, performance, and weight, and emphasizes that each state of the variable corresponds to a fuzzy number.
chunks.raw_text:
▶ Linguistic variables

▶ In reality fuzzy numbers represent values of linguistic variable
▶ Linguistic variable are those with fuzzy state:
  ▶ Temperature
  ▶ Performance
  ▶ Weight
  ▶ ...
▶ Each state of the variable is a fuzzy number
chunks.embedding_text: The slide introduces the concept of linguistic variables in fuzzy logic, explaining that fuzzy numbers represent values of these variables. It lists examples of linguistic variables with fuzzy states such as temperature, performance, and weight, and emphasizes that each state of the variable corresponds to a fuzzy number.

chunks.title: Fuzzy arithmetic
chunks.content: The slide explains how mathematical operators defined on real numbers (R) can be generalized to work with fuzzy numbers through two methods: Interval arithmetic and the Extension principle. It also mentions the use of alpha cuts in converting fuzzy sets to crisp sets and vice versa.
chunks.raw_text:
▶ Fuzzy arithmetic

▶ We can generalize mathematical operators defined on R to be applied to the space of fuzzy number by two ways:

▶ Interval arithmetic
▶ Recall that we can use alpha cuts to convert fuzzy set to crisp set back and forth

▶ Extension principle
▶ Operators are also functions defined from R x R to R
chunks.embedding_text: The slide explains how mathematical operators defined on real numbers (R) can be generalized to work with fuzzy numbers through two methods: Interval arithmetic and the Extension principle. It also mentions the use of alpha cuts in converting fuzzy sets to crisp sets and vice versa.

chunks.content: A method to generalize mathematical operations on real numbers to fuzzy numbers by using alpha cuts to convert fuzzy sets to crisp sets.
chunks.embedding_text: Interval arithmetic: A method to generalize mathematical operations on real numbers to fuzzy numbers by using alpha cuts to convert fuzzy sets to crisp sets.

chunks.content: A principle that extends mathematical operators, which are functions from R x R to R, to operate on fuzzy numbers.
chunks.embedding_text: Extension principle: A principle that extends mathematical operators, which are functions from R x R to R, to operate on fuzzy numbers.

chunks.content: A technique used to convert fuzzy sets into crisp sets at different levels of membership (alpha levels).
chunks.embedding_text: Alpha cuts: A technique used to convert fuzzy sets into crisp sets at different levels of membership (alpha levels).

chunks.title: Arithmetic operations on closed intervals
chunks.content: The slide explains how to perform arithmetic operations (addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division) on closed intervals. It provides formulas for these operations and notes that real numbers can be represented as intervals for these operations.
chunks.raw_text:
Arithmetic operations on closed intervals

[a, b] + [d, e] = [a + d, b + e],
[a, b] − [d, e] = [a − e, b − d],
[a, b] · [d, e] = [min(ad, ae, bd, be), max(ad, ae, bd, be)],
and, provided that 0 ∉ [d, e],
[a, b]/[d, e] = [a, b] · [1/e, 1/d]
= [min(a/d, a/e, b/d, b/e), max(a/d, a/e, b/d, b/e)].

Note That a real number r can be represented as [r, r], hence you can use operators above for numbers and intervals
chunks.embedding_text: The slide explains how to perform arithmetic operations (addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division) on closed intervals. It provides formulas for these operations and notes that real numbers can be represented as intervals for these operations.

chunks.content: An interval of real numbers including its endpoints, denoted as [a, b].
chunks.embedding_text: Closed Interval: An interval of real numbers including its endpoints, denoted as [a, b].

chunks.content: A method of calculating with intervals rather than single numbers.
chunks.embedding_text: Interval Arithmetic: A method of calculating with intervals rather than single numbers.

formula.formula: [a, b] · [d, e] = [min(ad, ae, bd, be), max(ad, ae, bd, be)]

embedding_text.formula: [a, b] · [d, e] = [min(ad, ae, bd, be), max(ad, ae, bd, be)]

formula.formula: [a, b] / [d, e] = [min(a/d, a/e, b/d, b/e), max(a/d, a/e, b/d, b/e)] where 0 ∉ [d, e]

embedding_text.formula: [a, b] / [d, e] = [min(a/d, a/e, b/d, b/e), max(a/d, a/e, b/d, b/e)] where 0 ∉ [d, e]

chunks.title: Arithmetic operations on closed intervals properties
chunks.content: The slide outlines fundamental properties of arithmetic operations on closed intervals, including commutativity, associativity, identity, distributivity, and inclusion monotonicity. It provides a detailed list of mathematical properties and conditions for operations such as addition, multiplication, and subtraction on closed intervals.
chunks.raw_text:
Arithmetic operations on closed intervals properties

1. A + B = B + A,
   A · B = B · A (commutativity).
2. (A + B) + C = A + (B + C)
   (A · B) · C = A · (B · C) (associativity).
3. A = 0 + A ≈ A + 0
   A = 1 · A = A · 1 (identity).
4. A · (B + C) ⊆ A · B + A · C (subdistributivity).
5. If b · c ≥ 0 for every b ∈ B and c ∈ C, then A · (B + C) = A · B + A · C (distributivity).
   Furthermore, if A = [a, a], then a · (B + C) = a · B + a · C.
6. 0 ∈ A - A and 1 ∈ A / A.
7. If A ⊆ E and B ⊆ F, then:
   A + B ⊆ E + F,
   A - B ⊆ E - F,
   A · B ⊆ E · F,
   A / B ⊆ E / F (inclusion monotonicity).
chunks.embedding_text: The slide outlines fundamental properties of arithmetic operations on closed intervals, including commutativity, associativity, identity, distributivity, and inclusion monotonicity. It provides a detailed list of mathematical properties and conditions for operations such as addition, multiplication, and subtraction on closed intervals.

chunks.content: The property that the order of operands does not affect the result of the operation.
chunks.embedding_text: Commutativity: The property that the order of operands does not affect the result of the operation.

chunks.content: The property that the grouping of operations does not affect the result.
chunks.embedding_text: Associativity: The property that the grouping of operations does not affect the result.

chunks.content: The property that there exists an element which, when combined with another element, leaves the latter unchanged.
chunks.embedding_text: Identity: The property that there exists an element which, when combined with another element, leaves the latter unchanged.

chunks.content: The property that multiplication distributes over addition in a way that the result of the multiplication is a subset of the sum of the individual multiplications.
chunks.embedding_text: Subdistributivity: The property that multiplication distributes over addition in a way that the result of the multiplication is a subset of the sum of the individual multiplications.

chunks.content: The property that multiplication distributes over addition when certain conditions are met.
chunks.embedding_text: Distributivity: The property that multiplication distributes over addition when certain conditions are met.

chunks.content: The property that if intervals A and B are subsets of intervals E and F respectively, then operations on A and B are subsets of the same operations on E and F.
chunks.embedding_text: Inclusion Monotonicity: The property that if intervals A and B are subsets of intervals E and F respectively, then operations on A and B are subsets of the same operations on E and F.
A + B = B + A
A · B = B · A
(A + B) + C = A + (B + C)
(A · B) · C = A · (B · C)

chunks.formula: A · (B + C) ⊆ A · B + A · C
chunks.embedding_text: A · (B + C) ⊆ A · B + A · C

chunks.formula: If b · c ≥ 0 for every b ∈ B and c ∈ C, then A · (B + C) = A · B + A · C
chunks.embedding_text: If b · c ≥ 0 for every b ∈ B and c ∈ C, then A · (B + C) = A · B + A · C

chunks.formula: If A = [a, a], then a · (B + C) = a · B + a · C
chunks.embedding_text: If A = [a, a], then a · (B + C) = a · B + a · C
0 ∈ A - A and 1 ∈ A / A

chunks.embedding_text: If A ⊆ E and B ⊆ F, then A + B ⊆ E + F

chunks.title: Fuzzy arithmetic based on interval arithmetic
chunks.content: The slide explains the method of performing fuzzy arithmetic operations by using interval arithmetic on alpha cuts of fuzzy numbers. It describes how to represent fuzzy numbers and apply interval arithmetic to obtain the resulting fuzzy number's alpha cuts.
chunks.raw_text:
Fuzzy arithmetic based on interval arithmetic

▶ Represent fuzzy numbers as their alpha cuts
▶ Apply interval arithmetic to get cuts of the result
α(A * B) = αA * αB
▶ You can generate the results easily (refer to lecture 2):
A * B = ∪ α∈[0,1] (α(A * B)).
chunks.embedding_text: The slide explains the method of performing fuzzy arithmetic operations by using interval arithmetic on alpha cuts of fuzzy numbers. It describes how to represent fuzzy numbers and apply interval arithmetic to obtain the resulting fuzzy number's alpha cuts.

chunks.title: Fuzzy arithmetic by using extension principle
chunks.content: The slide presents the extension principle applied to fuzzy arithmetic operations. It demonstrates how addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division are defined for fuzzy sets using supremum and minimum operations.
chunks.raw_text:
Fuzzy arithmetic by using extension principle

(A + B)(z) = sup min[A(x), B(y)],
z=x+y
(A - B)(z) = sup min[A(x), B(y)],
z=x-y
(A · B)(z) = sup min[A(x), B(y)],
z=x·y
(A / B)(z) = sup min[A(x), B(y)].
z=x/y
chunks.embedding_text: The slide presents the extension principle applied to fuzzy arithmetic operations. It demonstrates how addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division are defined for fuzzy sets using supremum and minimum operations.

chunks.content: A method to extend classical arithmetic operations to fuzzy sets by using the supremum of minimum membership values.
chunks.embedding_text: Extension Principle: A method to extend classical arithmetic operations to fuzzy sets by using the supremum of minimum membership values.

chunks.content: A branch of mathematics dealing with arithmetic operations on fuzzy sets.
chunks.embedding_text: Fuzzy Arithmetic: A branch of mathematics dealing with arithmetic operations on fuzzy sets.

chunks.title: Ordering of fuzzy numbers
chunks.content: The slide discusses the concept of ordering fuzzy numbers, highlighting that real numbers are fully ordered but fuzzy numbers are not. It introduces the idea of defining a partial order for fuzzy numbers using extension principles for min and max operations.
chunks.raw_text:
Ordering of fuzzy numbers

▶ Number in R are fully ordered, but clearly fuzzy numbers are not
▶ We can define a partial order of fuzzy number by extending the principle of min and max by using extension principle:

MIN(A, B)(z) = sup min[A(x), B(y)],
z=min(x,y)

MAX(A, B)(z) = sup min[A(x), B(y)]
z=max(x,y)
chunks.embedding_text: The slide discusses the concept of ordering fuzzy numbers, highlighting that real numbers are fully ordered but fuzzy numbers are not. It introduces the idea of defining a partial order for fuzzy numbers using extension principles for min and max operations.

chunks.title: Ordering of fuzzy numbers
chunks.content: The slide explains how fuzzy numbers can be ordered using minimum and maximum operations. It highlights that not all fuzzy numbers are comparable but most linguistic terms within a linguistic variable are comparable, providing an example of ordering linguistic terms from smallest to largest.
chunks.raw_text:
Ordering of fuzzy numbers:

▶ We can define:

A ≤ B iff MIN(A, B) = A or, alternatively,
A ≤ B iff MAX(A, B) = B

▶ Note that not all fuzzy numbers are comparable but most of linguistic terms of a linguistic variable are comparable, in our past example:

very small ≤ small ≤ medium ≤ large ≤ very large.
chunks.embedding_text: The slide explains how fuzzy numbers can be ordered using minimum and maximum operations. It highlights that not all fuzzy numbers are comparable but most linguistic terms within a linguistic variable are comparable, providing an example of ordering linguistic terms from smallest to largest.
